title: Swollen Hot Lump on Cat's Shoulder and Limping: Causes & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat limping with a hot shoulder lump? Learn about abscesses, urgency levels, and why a photo helps your vet diagnose the issue quickly.
content_html:
What is this lump?

A hot, painful lump on a cat's shoulder accompanied by a limp is frequently a feline abscess. This is a pocket of infection that usually forms after a puncture wound, such as a bite or scratch from another animal. The heat indicates that your cat's immune system is fighting an active infection. Other possibilities include a localized reaction to an injection or a soft tissue injury with significant inflammation.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While a localized abscess is generally not an immediate life-threatening emergency, the pain and infection can worsen quickly. If the abscess ruptures or if the infection enters the bloodstream (sepsis), the situation becomes more critical. You should contact your veterinarian for an appointment within the next 24 hours. If your cat is extremely lethargic or refuses to eat, seek urgent care.

Why a photo helps with triage

Providing your veterinarian with a clear, well-lit photo of the swelling and a short video of your cat walking can be incredibly helpful for remote triage. A photo allows the medical team to see the exact location and look for 'scabs' or puncture marks that indicate a bite. This helps them determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and prepares them for the necessary treatment, such as drainage or antibiotics, before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Should I try to drain the lump myself?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to squeeze or lance a lump at home. This is very painful for your cat and can push the infection deeper into the surrounding tissue.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relievers?
faqs.answer: Absolutely not. Human medications like Tylenol (Acetaminophen) or Advil (Ibuprofen) are toxic to cats and can be fatal.

faqs.question: What will the vet do for a hot lump?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam, possibly aspirate the lump to check for pus, and may need to sedate your cat to clean and drain the area and prescribe antibiotics.
